findings.title: All 4 stores hold business state only in memory, no persistence
findings.description: All four new Pinia stores (memory, todo, skills, activity) store their data exclusively in reactive refs with no persistence layer. Memories, todos, skills, and activities are all lost on page reload. The todo store is especially problematic because it supports addTodo/deleteTodo/toggleTodo as write operations that go nowhere -- the user performs actions that silently disappear. The memory and skills stores also support deleteMemory and toggleSkill which have the same ephemeral mutation problem.
findings.suggestion: Wire these stores to either (a) localStorage/IndexedDB for client-side persistence, (b) IPC calls to the Electron main process with SQLite-backed storage, or (c) an API endpoint. At minimum, todos created by the user MUST survive page refresh. For the read-only panels (memory, activity, skills), implement the data-fetching path so the stores actually receive data from context-engine / skills-engine backends.

findings.title: UI-only horizontal slice: no backend data source wired for any panel
findings.description: setMemories, setActivities, setSummary, setSkills, and setTodos are the only entry points for populating these stores, but no production code ever calls them -- only test files do. All four settings panels will render empty states permanently. This is a horizontal-only change: four complete UI panels with no vertical connection to any data source (context-engine, skills-engine, or any IPC/API). The stores expose setter functions that are dead code in production.
findings.suggestion: Implement at least one vertical slice: pick one panel (e.g. memory) and wire it end-to-end from context-engine through IPC to the store. For others, add onMounted hooks in each settings page that fetch data via IPC calls to the Electron main process, or via WebSocket/HTTP API when running in stage-web.

findings.title: Todo store has unbounded growth with no size cap
findings.description: addTodo appends to the todos array without any maximum size check. Since this is an in-memory-only store, a user (or automated process calling addTodo) can grow the array indefinitely, consuming unbounded memory. Similarly, the memory store's setMemories accepts arbitrary-length arrays. The activity store's setActivities has the same issue. None of the stores implement pagination, virtual scrolling, or size caps.
findings.suggestion: Add a MAX_ITEMS constant (e.g. 1000 for todos, configurable for memories). In addTodo, check against the cap before inserting. For the list views, implement virtual scrolling or pagination if the data set can be large. This becomes critical once persistence is added and real data flows in.

findings.title: UI types mirror context-engine types but no shared contract
findings.description: The comment explicitly states these types 'mirror the context-engine types', but there is no shared interface, import, or contract test validating that the UI types actually match the backend types. If context-engine changes its MemoryEntry shape, these UI types will silently drift. The same applies to ActivityEntryUI, SkillUI, and TodoUI -- all are standalone definitions with no contract binding to their backend counterparts.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared contract package (or add to an existing shared package like stage-shared) defining the canonical types. Both context-engine and stage-ui should import from the shared contract. Alternatively, add a contract test that imports both types and verifies structural compatibility.

findings.title: Non-null assertion on selectedMemory in delete button handler
findings.code_snippet: <Button @click="memoryStore.deleteMemory(selectedMemory!.id)">
findings.description: The delete button in the detail panel uses a non-null assertion (selectedMemory!.id). While the v-if="selectedMemory" guard on the parent div provides runtime safety, the non-null assertion bypasses TypeScript's null checking. If the template structure is ever refactored (e.g. the v-if is moved or removed), this becomes a runtime error with no compile-time warning.
findings.suggestion: Use optional chaining with an early return guard: `selectedMemory?.id && memoryStore.deleteMemory(selectedMemory.id)`, or extract the delete handler into a named function in <script setup> that checks for null before calling deleteMemory.

findings.title: formatDate and formatDuration duplicated across components
findings.description: The formatDate function is duplicated identically in MemoryManager.vue (line 27) and TodoPanel.vue (line 32). The formatDuration function in ActivityTimeline.vue is a standalone implementation. These formatting utilities should be shared. Additionally, formatDate uses toLocaleDateString() without locale parameter, which may produce inconsistent results across environments.
findings.suggestion: Extract formatDate and formatDuration into a shared composable (e.g. packages/stage-ui/src/composables/use-formatters.ts) or a utils module. Pass the i18n locale to toLocaleDateString for consistent formatting.

findings.title: All UI types for 4 unrelated domains packed into single memory.ts
findings.description: The file packages/stage-ui/src/types/memory.ts contains type definitions for four distinct domains: memory, todo, activity, and skills. The file is named 'memory.ts' but contains unrelated types. This violates SRP at the file level and will cause confusion as these domains evolve independently. The todo.ts store imports 'TodoFilter' and 'TodoUI' from '../../types/memory' which is misleading.
findings.suggestion: Split into domain-specific type files: types/memory.ts, types/todo.ts, types/activity.ts, types/skills.ts. Each store module would import from its corresponding type file. Alternatively, create a types/index.ts barrel that re-exports from individual files.

findings.title: JSDoc claims to 'mirror' context-engine types without enforcement
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * UI-layer types for memory management panels.
 * Mirror the context-engine types for use in browser/renderer.
 */
findings.description: The JSDoc claims these types 'mirror the context-engine types', but there is no import, schema validation, or type-level link to the actual context-engine types. If context-engine types evolve, this file will silently diverge while the comment still claims they are mirrors. This will mislead future maintainers into assuming type parity that does not exist.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) import and re-export the context-engine types with Pick/Omit to enforce the mirror relationship, or (b) reword the comment to 'Standalone UI-layer types for memory management panels, inspired by context-engine domain types' to avoid implying a live mirror.

findings.title: TodoUI allows illegal completed/completedAt combinations
findings.code_snippet:
export interface TodoUI {
  id: string
  title: string
  completed: boolean
  createdAt: number
  completedAt: number | null
}
findings.description: TodoUI allows completed=true with completedAt=null and completed=false with completedAt=<timestamp>. The toggleTodo() store function correctly maintains the invariant, but the type itself does not prevent invalid combinations. A discriminated union on completed status would make illegal states unrepresentable.
findings.suggestion: Replace with discriminated union: type TodoUI = { id: string; title: string; createdAt: number } & ({ completed: false; completedAt: null } | { completed: true; completedAt: number }). Alternatively, if the backend Todo type is the canonical shape and UI must mirror it, document this as a known type gap and add a runtime guard in setTodos().

findings.title: No test for whitespace-only search query in memory store
findings.description: The memory store's filteredMemories computed property uses .trim() to ignore whitespace-only search queries (memory.ts line 19), but no test verifies that setting searchQuery to whitespace-only (e.g., '   ') still returns all memories. This is a meaningful boundary condition for search input handling.
findings.suggestion: Add a test case: set searchQuery to '   ' (whitespace only) and verify filteredMemories returns all memories, confirming the trim() guard works correctly.

findings.title: SkillUI.category and version are plain strings
findings.code_snippet:
export interface SkillUI {
  id: string
  name: string
  category: string
  version: string
  description: string
  source: 'builtin' | 'user'
  tags: string[]
  active: boolean
}
findings.description: SkillUI.category is a plain string while the same file uses literal unions for MemoryCategory, TodoFilter, and SkillUI.source. The inconsistency suggests category should also be a literal union. SkillUI.version is also plain string where a semver-like branded type or at minimum a SkillVersion type alias would express intent.
findings.suggestion: Define a SkillCategory literal union (matching known categories from the skills engine) and use it for SkillUI.category. At minimum, add a type alias: type SkillCategory = string to mark the intent for future narrowing.

findings.title: Todo clearCompleted untested for all-completed and none-completed
findings.description: The clearCompleted tests only cover the mixed case (some active, some completed). Two boundary conditions are missing: (1) clearing when ALL todos are completed should result in an empty list, and (2) clearing when NO todos are completed should leave the list unchanged.
findings.suggestion: Add two tests: (1) 'should clear all todos when all are completed' -- add todos, complete all, clearCompleted, assert empty. (2) 'should do nothing when no todos are completed' -- add active-only todos, clearCompleted, assert unchanged length.

findings.title: Skills filteredSkills has no test for zero-match search result
findings.description: The filteredSkills tests verify that matching skills are returned, but no test checks the case where the search query matches zero skills. Verifying that a search with no matches returns an empty array is a standard boundary condition for filter/search functionality.
findings.suggestion: Add test: set skills to a list, set searchQuery to 'zzz_nonexistent', and assert filteredSkills returns an empty array.

findings.title: Duplicate duration formatting logic and magic numbers within ActivityTimeline.vue
findings.description: The formattedDuration computed property (lines 12-16) and the formatDuration function (lines 25-31) contain near-identical hour/minute extraction logic. The only difference is that formatDuration omits the hours portion when hours is 0, while formattedDuration always includes it (showing '0h Xm'). This is both a duplication issue and uses magic numbers (3600000 and 60000) repeated 4 times. Named constants would improve readability and reduce the chance of a typo.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported useMemoryModuleStore or its public methods
findings.description: The project convention is JSDoc on all exported interfaces and functions. This store exports 7 public methods and 4 reactive properties with zero documentation. A future maintainer will not know the intended contract (e.g., does deleteMemory also clear selection if the deleted item was selected?).
findings.suggestion: Add at minimum a store-level JSDoc describing purpose, and JSDoc on non-obvious methods like deleteMemory (which has a side-effect on selectedMemory) and selectMemory.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported useTodoModuleStore or its public methods
findings.description: Same missing-JSDoc issue. The store has 6 public methods and 2 computed properties with no documentation. Methods like addTodo silently discard empty/whitespace-only input (a behavior worth documenting), and toggleTodo has a timestamp side-effect on completedAt.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the store and its public methods, especially addTodo (documents the trim + empty-reject behavior) and toggleTodo (documents the completedAt timestamp toggling).

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported useSkillsModuleStore or its public methods
findings.description: Store exports 4 public methods and 2 computed properties with no JSDoc. The filteredSkills computed searches across name, description, and tags -- this multi-field search logic is non-obvious and worth documenting.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc, particularly on filteredSkills (documenting the multi-field search across name/description/tags) and toggleSkill.

findings.title: No JSDoc on exported useActivityModuleStore or its public methods
findings.description: Store exports 3 public methods and 3 computed properties with no JSDoc. The activities ref is declared but never consumed by any computed property in this store, which would confuse a maintainer without documentation explaining its intended use.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc to the store explaining its role, and document why activities is stored separately from todaySummary (are they consumed differently by the UI?).

findings.title: getSkillById returns mutable reference to store entry
findings.description: getSkillById() returns a direct reference to the array element in the reactive store. External code can mutate the skill object without going through toggleSkill() or setSkills(), bypassing any future validation. This is the same mutable-reference leak pattern found in prior reviews (SkillRegistry, CronService, ChannelRegistry).
findings.suggestion: Return a spread copy: return skills.value.find(s => s.id === id) ? { ...skills.value.find(s => s.id === id)! } : undefined. Or use a Readonly<SkillUI> return type to signal immutability intent.

findings.title: Activity store personalNote field never asserted in tests
findings.description: The DailySummaryUI type has a personalNote field, and the createSummary factory includes it, but no test ever asserts that personalNote is preserved after setSummary. Since setSummary does a shallow spread copy ({ ...summary }), this should be verified to confirm no field is lost during the copy.
findings.suggestion: In the 'should set today summary' test, add an assertion: expect(store.todaySummary?.personalNote).toBe('Productive day!') to verify the personalNote field survives the spread copy.

findings.title: No test for filtering/operations on empty collections
findings.description: Across all four stores, filter/computed tests always populate the collection first. No test verifies that filtering an empty collection works correctly (returns empty array, not undefined or error). This is a universal boundary condition: filteredMemories on empty memories, filteredTodos on empty todos, filteredSkills on empty skills.
findings.suggestion: Add a simple test per store: e.g., 'should return empty array when filtering with no items set'. Verify filteredMemories/filteredTodos/filteredSkills returns [] on a fresh store with a filter/search applied.

findings.title: Identical formatDate function duplicated across two components
findings.description: The same formatDate function (identical body) appears in both MemoryManager.vue (line 27) and TodoPanel.vue (line 32). Consolidating into a shared composable or utility now prevents further copy-paste as more panels are added.
findings.suggestion: Extract to a shared utility (e.g. packages/stage-ui/src/utils/format.ts) and import in each component.

findings.title: All UI interfaces lack readonly modifiers
findings.description: All 7 interfaces use mutable fields. While Vue/Pinia reactivity requires mutable refs at the store level, the type definitions themselves could use readonly fields to express that consumers should not directly mutate entries. The stores already use immutable update patterns (spread copies).
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all interface fields. Pinia ref<T[]> wraps with UnwrapRef which preserves readonly intent while allowing reactive tracking. Informational for walking skeleton.

findings.title: Timestamp and duration fields use bare number throughout
findings.code_snippet:
createdAt: number
...
timestamp: number
...
durationMs: number
...
totalWorkDurationMs: number
findings.description: Multiple fields across interfaces use bare number for timestamps and durations. The Ms suffix convention is a good start but a branded type like Milliseconds or UnixTimestampMs would prevent mixing units and catch assignment errors at compile time. This is a codebase-wide pattern flagged in prior reviews.
findings.suggestion: Define branded types: type UnixTimestampMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'UnixTimestampMs' }; type DurationMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'DurationMs' }. Low priority for walking skeleton.

findings.title: Empty state template pattern repeated across 4 new components
findings.code_snippet: {{ t('...empty') }}
findings.description: All 4 new components plus the existing McpManager share an identical empty state pattern: a centered flex container with neutral background, an icon, and a text message. Only the icon class and i18n key differ. At 5 occurrences this crosses the 3-use extraction threshold and is a natural candidate for a reusable Vue component.
findings.suggestion: Extract an EmptyState.vue component with icon and message props. Eliminates ~8 lines per usage site and ensures consistent empty-state styling.

findings.title: Unnecessary vi.mock('vue-i18n') in all four test files
findings.description: All four test files mock vue-i18n, but none of the four source modules import or use vue-i18n. The mock is unnecessary boilerplate likely copy-pasted from another test file.
findings.suggestion: Remove the vi.mock('vue-i18n') block and its rationale comment from all four test files. Only mock vue-i18n in test files whose source modules actually import it.

findings.title: Misleading test name: 'called with null' but calls clearSelection()
findings.description: The test is named 'should clear selection when called with null' but the test body calls store.clearSelection(), not selectMemory(null). The test is inside describe('selectMemory') block, making it doubly misleading.
findings.suggestion: Rename to 'should clear selection via clearSelection()' and move it to a separate describe('clearSelection') block.

findings.title: Template section comments restate visually obvious structure
findings.code_snippet:
<!-- Header with stats -->
<!-- Search and filter -->
<!-- Memory list -->
<!-- Empty state -->
<!-- Detail panel -->
findings.description: All four Vue components use HTML comments like '<!-- Header with stats -->' to mark template sections. Most restate what is immediately visually obvious from the template structure. 17 such comments total across 4 components.
findings.suggestion: Minor style observation. Consider removing the most obvious ones and keeping only those that mark non-obvious sections.

findings.title: Toggle and filter buttons lack accessible labels
findings.code_snippet:
<button
  :class="[...]"
  @click="skillsStore.toggleSkill(skill.id)"
>
findings.description: The skill toggle button, memory delete button, todo checkbox, and category filter buttons all lack aria-label attributes. The toggle switch in SkillsManager.vue is entirely visual with no text content or aria-label, making it inaccessible to screen readers.
findings.suggestion: Add :aria-label attributes to all icon-only buttons. For the toggle: :aria-label="`Toggle ${skill.name}`". For delete buttons: :aria-label="`Delete ${memory.content.slice(0, 30)}`".

positive_observations: Clean separation of concerns: Pinia stores handle state logic, Vue components handle presentation, types are defined separately.
Proper use of storeToRefs for reactive destructuring from Pinia stores -- avoids reactivity loss.
All user-facing strings properly externalized to i18n YAML files with parameterized interpolation.
Immutable update patterns used throughout stores (spread operator, filter, map) instead of direct array mutation.
Settings pages correctly use the settingsEntry route meta pattern for automatic discovery in the settings layout.
Empty state handling present in all four components with appropriate fallback UI.
Good input validation in addTodo: trims whitespace and rejects empty strings before creating a todo.
Store tests are thorough and use real Pinia instances -- no mocking of store internals.
Zero forbidden patterns (TODO/FIXME/HACK/XXX/PLACEHOLDER/TEMP) found across all new files.
All 4 Pinia stores are synchronous-only with no I/O operations -- error-free-by-design pattern.
All 4 Vue components are purely presentational with no async operations, no fetch calls, no JSON.parse -- no error handling needed.
Memory store correctly nullifies selectedMemory when deleted item was selected -- avoids stale reference bugs.
All store modules are well-structured with short functions (< 15 lines each), low cyclomatic complexity (1-3), and maximum nesting depth of 2.
Consistent use of early returns and guard clauses (e.g., addTodo trims and exits early, filteredSkills returns early when no query).
Vue components maintain clean separation: stores handle state logic, components handle presentation only.
Template code uses the project-standard :class array pattern for readable UnoCSS class grouping.
MemoryCategory is a proper 5-value literal union, improving over backend MemoryEntry.category. Good type narrowing at UI boundary.
Factory functions (createMemory, createTodo, createSkill, createSummary) with override support make tests readable and maintainable.
Edge cases like nonexistent IDs and toggle round-trips are tested across stores.
State reset tests verify all state fields return to initial values.
Store modules follow consistent Pinia composition API pattern with clear separation: state refs, computed getters, mutation functions, resetState.
